title: Grip Strength: The Complete Guide
description: Everything clinicians, operators and researchers need to know about grip strength: why it matters, how to measure it, what the evidence says, and how to use it for falls prevention.
author: Able Care Clinical Team
excerpt: Grip strength is one of the most studied biomarkers in clinical medicine. It predicts falls, frailty, sarcopenia, cardiovascular disease and all-cause mortality. This is the definitive guide from the clinical team at Able Care.

blocks.headline: Grip strength: the complete guide.
blocks.subtitle: Why it matters, how to measure it, what the evidence says. The definitive resource from the clinical team at Able Care.

blocks.body:
What is grip strength?

Grip strength is the maximal isometric force your hand and forearm muscles can produce when squeezing a dynamometer. It is quick to measure (under 30 seconds), non-invasive and requires minimal patient cooperation. That simplicity is part of why it has become one of the most widely studied biomarkers in geriatric and population health research.

But grip strength is not just a hand measurement. It is a validated proxy for whole-body muscle strength, and its predictive power extends far beyond the upper limb.

Why grip strength matters

The evidence base for grip strength is extensive and spans multiple clinical domains.

Falls and frailty

Low grip strength is an independent predictor of falls in older adults. It is also one of the five criteria in the Fried frailty phenotype, the most widely used clinical frailty definition. A person with declining grip strength is more likely to fall, more likely to be hospitalized after a fall and less likely to recover independently.

Mortality

The Lancet published a landmark study in 2015 (the PURE study) showing that grip strength predicts all-cause mortality more reliably than systolic blood pressure. This finding held across 17 countries and nearly 140,000 participants.

Sarcopenia

The European Working Group on Sarcopenia in Older People (EWGSOP2) uses grip strength as the primary screening criterion for sarcopenia. Low grip strength (below 27 kg for men, below 16 kg for women) triggers further investigation. As GLP-1 therapies become more widespread, monitoring muscle loss alongside weight loss is becoming a clinical priority.

Cardiovascular and respiratory disease

Research links low grip strength to increased risk of cardiovascular events, heart failure and COPD exacerbations. For clinicians managing multi-morbid older adults, grip strength provides a quick, integrated signal of physiological reserve.

Cancer and surgical outcomes

In oncology, grip strength is an emerging endpoint for assessing treatment-related muscle wasting and predicting post-surgical recovery. Low pre-operative grip strength is associated with longer hospital stays and higher complication rates.

How is grip strength measured?

Traditionally, the Jamar hydraulic dynamometer has been the clinical standard. The patient squeezes the device three times with each hand, and the highest reading is recorded. This approach is reliable, but it has significant limitations: no digital data capture, no longitudinal tracking and no integration with care systems.

Digital grip strength platforms like the GripAble sensor capture the same clinical-grade measurement with Bluetooth connectivity. Data flows automatically into a structured workflow, is compared against age- and sex-matched normative data, and can be tracked longitudinally. This is the approach used by Able Assess.

What is a normal grip strength?

Normal grip strength varies by age, sex and dominant hand. The EWGSOP2 cut-offs for probable sarcopenia are below 27 kg for men and below 16 kg for women. Clinically meaningful decline is relative to an individual's baseline, which is why longitudinal tracking matters as much as single-point measurement.

Grip strength in falls prevention

Grip strength is most predictive when combined with other functional measures. The 2022 World Guidelines for Falls Prevention recommend multifactorial screening that includes strength, balance, gait and mobility assessments. Able Assess captures grip strength alongside sit-to-stand, gait speed and Timed Up and Go in a single five-minute workflow.

Grip strength in clinical trials

Grip strength is increasingly used as a functional endpoint in clinical trials, particularly in oncology, GLP-1 muscle-loss monitoring and sarcopenia interventions. Digital capture enables remote, standardized measurement for decentralized trials.

citations.title: Prognostic value of grip strength: findings from the Prospective Urban Rural Epidemiology (PURE) study

blocks.heading: Frequently asked questions about grip strength

faqs.question: What is a normal grip strength for my age?
faqs.answer: Normal grip strength varies by age, sex and dominant hand. As a general guide, the EWGSOP2 defines low grip strength as below 27 kg for men and below 16 kg for women. Digital platforms like Able Assess compare your result against age- and sex-matched normative data automatically.

faqs.question: How is grip strength linked to falls risk?
faqs.answer: Grip strength is a validated proxy for whole-body muscle strength. Low grip strength correlates strongly with lower-body strength and overall functional capacity. A person with declining grip strength is more likely to fall and more likely to be hospitalized after a fall.

faqs.question: Can non-clinical staff measure grip strength?
faqs.answer: Yes. Digital grip strength platforms like Able Assess are designed for non-clinical staff. The workflow guides the user through a standardized protocol, captures the data automatically and scores against normative data. No clinical qualification is required.

faqs.question: What is the difference between a Jamar dynamometer and a digital dynamometer?
faqs.answer: A Jamar dynamometer is analogue: it provides a single force reading with no digital data capture, no longitudinal tracking and no integration with care systems. A digital dynamometer like the GripAble sensor captures the same clinical-grade measurement with automatic data recording, normative comparison and workflow integration.

faqs.question: Why does the Lancet study on grip strength matter?
faqs.answer: The 2015 PURE study, published in The Lancet, found that grip strength predicts all-cause mortality more reliably than systolic blood pressure. This finding, replicated across 17 countries, established grip strength as one of the most powerful single biomarkers in population health.

faqs.question: Is grip strength useful on its own or does it need to be combined with other tests?
faqs.answer: Grip strength is a powerful standalone predictor, but its value increases substantially when combined with other functional measures. The 2022 World Guidelines recommend multifactorial screening including strength, balance, gait and mobility. Able Assess captures all four in a single five-minute workflow.

body:
## What is grip strength?

Grip strength is the maximal isometric force your hand and forearm muscles can produce when squeezing a dynamometer. It is quick to measure (under 30 seconds), non-invasive and requires minimal patient cooperation. That simplicity is part of why it has become one of the most widely studied biomarkers in geriatric and population health research.

But grip strength is not just a hand measurement. It is a validated proxy for whole-body muscle strength, and its predictive power extends far beyond the upper limb.

## Why grip strength matters

The evidence base for grip strength is extensive and spans multiple clinical domains.

### Falls and frailty

Low grip strength is an independent predictor of falls in older adults. It is also one of the five criteria in the Fried frailty phenotype, the most widely used clinical frailty definition. A person with declining grip strength is more likely to fall, more likely to be hospitalized after a fall and less likely to recover independently.

### Mortality

The Lancet published a landmark study in 2015 (the PURE study) showing that grip strength predicts all-cause mortality more reliably than systolic blood pressure. This finding held across 17 countries and nearly 140,000 participants. Since then, multiple large-scale cohort studies have replicated the association.

### Sarcopenia

The European Working Group on Sarcopenia in Older People (EWGSOP2) uses grip strength as the primary screening criterion for sarcopenia. Low grip strength (below 27 kg for men, below 16 kg for women) triggers further investigation. As GLP-1 therapies become more widespread, monitoring muscle loss alongside weight loss is becoming a clinical priority, and grip strength is the frontline measurement.

### Cardiovascular and respiratory disease

Research links low grip strength to increased risk of cardiovascular events, heart failure and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) exacerbations. For clinicians managing multi-morbid older adults, grip strength provides a quick, integrated signal of physiological reserve.

### Cancer and surgical outcomes

In oncology, grip strength is an emerging endpoint for assessing treatment-related muscle wasting and predicting post-surgical recovery. Low pre-operative grip strength is associated with longer hospital stays, higher complication rates and poorer rehabilitation outcomes.

## How is grip strength measured?

### The traditional approach

For decades, the standard instrument has been the Jamar hydraulic dynamometer. The patient squeezes the device three times with each hand at a standardized handle position (typically position II). The highest reading is recorded in kilograms. This approach is reliable, but it has significant limitations: no digital data capture, no automatic normative comparison, no longitudinal tracking and no integration with electronic health records.

### Digital grip strength platforms

Digital dynamometers like the GripAble sensor capture the same clinical-grade measurement with Bluetooth connectivity. The data flows automatically into a structured workflow, is compared against age- and sex-matched normative data, and can be tracked longitudinally. This is the approach used by Able Assess.

For operators running screenings across multiple sites, the difference is significant. A digital platform delivers consistent measurement, automatic scoring and a data trail that is defensible to payers and regulators, without depending on clinical staff to interpret and record results manually.

## What is a normal grip strength?

Normal grip strength varies by age, sex and dominant hand. The EWGSOP2 cut-offs for probable sarcopenia are below 27 kg for men and below 16 kg for women. However, clinically meaningful decline is relative to an individual's baseline, which is why longitudinal tracking matters as much as single-point measurement.

Population-level normative data is available from large cohort studies and is built into digital platforms like Able Assess, allowing instant comparison at the point of care.

## Grip strength in falls prevention

On its own, grip strength is a powerful predictor. But its value increases substantially when combined with other functional measures. The 2022 World Guidelines for Falls Prevention recommend multifactorial screening that includes strength, balance, gait and mobility assessments.

Able Assess captures grip strength alongside sit-to-stand, gait speed and Timed Up and Go in a single five-minute workflow. This multifactorial approach is more predictive than any single test and aligns with CDC STEADI and NICE 2025 guidance.

## Grip strength in clinical trials

Grip strength is increasingly used as a functional endpoint in clinical trials, particularly in oncology, GLP-1 muscle-loss monitoring and sarcopenia interventions. Digital capture enables remote, standardized measurement, making grip strength viable as an endpoint in decentralized trials.

## Key takeaways

- Grip strength is a validated proxy for whole-body strength and a strong predictor of falls, frailty and mortality.
- The Lancet (2015) found grip strength predicts all-cause mortality more reliably than blood pressure.
- The EWGSOP2 defines low grip strength as below 27 kg (men) and below 16 kg (women).
- Digital dynamometers enable automatic scoring, normative comparison and longitudinal tracking.
- Grip strength is most predictive when combined with other functional measures in a multifactorial screening.
- NICE 2025 and CDC STEADI recommend objective, standardized falls screening using validated tools.
